Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, but with work and everything else going on, it rarely stuck. I'm a visual learner, so I'd make diagrams and color-coded notes when I could, but finding consistent time was a constant struggle. Most weeks I'd end up cramming whenever I had a free moment between shifts.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have intense bursts of activity right before deadlines. I wanted to be more consistent, but between financial stress and anxiety, regular schedules felt impossible to maintain. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced structure onto my studying.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline or seeing an email notification about something due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my attendance was so spotty. The anxiety of not knowing what was happening in the course would build up until I had to check. Occasionally I'd log in after a mindfulness session when I felt calmer and more capable.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more routine, especially early in the semester when I was determined to do better. I'd set phone reminders and try to check in after my morning coffee. But whenever a crisis hit—money problems, health stuff, anxiety spiraling—that routine would completely fall apart. It never became truly automatic for me.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best visually. I watched over 200 minutes of them because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. The discussion posts were also helpful because seeing how other students understood concepts gave me different perspectives. Visual diagrams and flowcharts in the course materials really clicked with my brain.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials, especially PDFs that were just walls of text. With my visual learning style, those felt overwhelming and inefficient. I'd also delay the discussion posts sometimes because engaging with others required mental energy I didn't always have. When my anxiety was high, anything that required active participation felt like too much.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams. Visual content that I could screenshot and annotate was always valuable to me. I also appreciated anything that helped reduce my anxiety about the course—like clear instructions or examples. If something felt like busywork without clear purpose, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're juggling 14 online courses with limited resources and high anxiety, you have to prioritize ruthlessly. Required activities got done because they affected my grade, but optional ones usually got sacrificed. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping them, which just added to my stress.
Q9: Absolutely, I studied offline constantly. I'd download videos and watch them on my phone during bus rides to work since my internet at home isn't always reliable. I made physical flashcards with drawings and diagrams because that's how information sticks for me. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my kitchen table, not logged into anything.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or redrawing concept maps without touching the LMS. I'd study with a classmate at the library using our combined notes, which wouldn't show up anywhere. Sometimes I'd rewatch a downloaded lecture three or four times offline while doing breathing exercises to manage my anxiety. The LMS only saw a fraction of my actual study time.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd download everything I could in one session—videos, PDFs, slides—then work with them offline for days. With my limited resources, I couldn't always count on having stable internet when I needed to study. One download session might fuel a whole week of offline studying that looked like nothing in the system.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike to the point where even opening the LMS felt overwhelming. Financial stress made it hard to focus on academics when I was worried about rent. During those times, even the mindfulness exercises I'd learned could only do so much to keep me functional.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by having 14 courses to manage, the LMS became something I avoided. Confusion about assignment requirements would trigger my anxiety, making me procrastinate even more. Fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully—I'd log in but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away from coursework.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or replaying anxious thoughts. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really reading them, just going through the motions. My body was present but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they showed as 'activity.'
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially discussion posts and complex assignments. When my anxiety was high, starting anything felt paralyzing—I'd know it was important but feel frozen. Sometimes I'd avoid things because I was afraid of doing them wrong and that fear was worse than the deadline pressure. The mindfulness techniques helped sometimes, but they couldn't always break through the avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have bursts of intense activity in the 24-48 hours before something was due. My response time averaging almost 20 hours shows how last-minute I usually was. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all during my worst periods. They were stressful but necessary for someone like me.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would boost my mood and make me more likely to engage with the next thing. But critical feedback, even constructive, could trigger anxiety spirals that made me avoid the course for days. I needed to be in the right headspace to receive feedback without it derailing me. The mindfulness practice helped me process feedback more calmly over time.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having 14 courses meant I was constantly context-switching, which made deep engagement difficult. Clear deadlines and organized modules helped me prioritize, but when things weren't clearly laid out, my anxiety made it hard to figure out what to do. I needed more structure than was sometimes provided.
Q19: Visual elements like diagrams, video content, and clear navigation made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was dense text-based content and unclear expectations for assignments. When I couldn't quickly see what was required or how to succeed, I'd freeze up. Flexibility in deadlines would have helped someone in my situation enormously.
Q20: I can totally relate to that pattern. Sometimes you're desperately trying to figure things out, clicking through everything, watching videos, but you're so overwhelmed or confused that you can't actually produce quality work. High activity might mean someone is struggling, searching for understanding, dealing with anxiety that prevents them from completing things. Activity doesn't equal productivity when your mental state is compromised.
Q21: That partially reflects my experience, actually. I did a lot of learning offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. My exam score wasn't great, but I passed, and much of what got me there happened away from the LMS. Someone might look disengaged online but be studying intensively in ways the system can't track. Low resources sometimes mean finding alternative study methods.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, time spent on platform, and completion of online tasks. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just having something open, and the emotional state behind the activity. They can't tell if someone watched a video mindfully or while having a panic attack. They miss the human context entirely.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm curious, when concepts click and I want to understand more. For me, engagement looks like making visual notes, discussing ideas with others, applying concepts to real life. It's a mental and emotional state, not just clicking buttons on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels, my financial stress, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope better toward the end. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with physical notes and diagrams, or the peer study sessions. It can't show that despite my low attendance and mediocre scores, I was fighting hard just to stay enrolled and pass. My resilience was low but I kept trying anyway.
